Q1: I organized my study routine around listening and re-listening to lecture recordings, which really suits how I learn best. I'd typically dedicate about 17 hours a week, spreading it across several sessions rather than cramming everything into one day. Since I'm 26 and have been through a few academic challenges before, I've learned that consistency matters more than intensity. I'd often study in the evenings when I could focus on audio materials without distractions.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I tried to maintain some regularity, but honestly, deadlines drove a lot of my scheduling decisions. When multiple assignments overlapped, I'd shift my hours around significantly. I've gotten better at adapting rather than sticking rigidly to a schedule that doesn't work.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check for new lecture recordings or to see if any announcements had been posted. Sometimes it was just to verify deadlines or download materials I needed for offline study. I'd also pop in to see if there were responses to discussion posts, though I didn't post that frequently myself. The notification emails often prompted me to check in too.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic, especially in the mornings with my coffee. I'd built it into my routine of checking what needed attention for the day. It happened gradually as I realized the lecture videos were being uploaded on a predictable schedule. That predictability helped make it feel like a natural part of my week rather than an extra task.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I watched nearly 230 minutes worth because hearing concepts explained helped them stick in ways reading never could. I'd often play them while taking notes, sometimes rewinding to catch things I missed. The ability to pause and replay was invaluable for understanding complex topics.
Q6: I tended to skip over supplementary reading materials and some of the written resources. If something wasn't audio-based or didn't directly connect to assignments, I'd often delay looking at it. I also didn't engage much with discussion forums beyond a couple of posts because I found them less helpful than listening to lectures. Time constraints meant I had to prioritize what worked best for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand the material or prepare for assessments. I valued anything I could listen to repeatedly or that offered clear explanations of concepts. If I could see how it connected to my exam preparation or assignments, I was much more motivated to engage. Practical relevance was key for me.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was less pressure attached to them. I'd approach required activities with more urgency and focus, while optional ones I'd only explore if I had extra time or genuine curiosity. Sometimes optional materials were actually quite valuable, but without the deadline pressure, they'd slip down my priority list. I wish I'd engaged with more of them, honestly.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during commutes or while doing household tasks. I also used my own notes extensively, rewriting and organizing information in ways that helped me process it. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with friends outside the course, which was incredibly helpful.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't reflect it. For instance, I'd download a video once and then replay it multiple times offline over several days. I also spent hours reviewing my handwritten notes and creating audio recordings of myself summarizing key concepts. All that meaningful learning happened completely off-platform.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my primary study method. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue studying them offline for extended periods. The video lectures especially I'd download and watch repeatedly without logging back in. It was more convenient for my schedule and let me study in places without reliable internet access.
Q12: There were moments mid-semester when juggling four online courses became overwhelming, and my engagement with this particular course dipped. My attendance percentage reflects that I couldn't always show up to everything. During those times, I had to make tough choices about where to focus my energy. But my resilience kicked in, and I'd always find my way back to the material.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage patterns. When I was exhausted from managing multiple courses, I'd do the minimum online interaction and rely more on offline study methods. Confusion sometimes led me to spend more time on videos, rewinding and re-watching sections. But I've learned to manage these challenges without letting stress overwhelm me.
Q14: There were times when I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I was just too tired to focus properly, so I'd be technically present but mentally elsewhere. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to other concerns. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd register as activity.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered, which is probably why my assignment completion sat around 67%. Sometimes it was because I felt uncertain about what was expected, and that uncertainty made starting feel harder. Other times I simply prioritized activities that felt more aligned with how I learn. I'd tell myself I'd catch up, and sometimes I did, sometimes I didn't.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me and really shaped when I'd be most active on the LMS. I'd often have bursts of activity in the days leading up to due dates, downloading materials and checking requirements. Without deadlines, I might have spread my engagement more evenly, but the pressure helped me focus. My response time averaging around 14 hours shows I wasn't always immediate but I got things done.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, especially when it was constructive and specific. Positive feedback motivated me to maintain my approach, while critical feedback made me revisit materials and try different strategies. I appreciated when instructors explained what I could improve because it gave me direction. It made me feel like my effort was being noticed and guided.
Q18: The course structure with regular video uploads worked well for my auditory learning style. Having a predictable rhythm of content release helped me plan my study sessions. However, the structure could have been more accommodating for different learning preferences. When things were clearly organized and expectations were transparent, I found it much easier to stay engaged.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement easier because it matched how I learn best. However, the emphasis on written discussion posts felt harder for me since that's not my natural mode of expression. The low attendance requirements actually worked in my favor because I could engage with recordings on my own schedule. More audio-based interaction options would have helped me participate more actively.
Q20: I think students might show high LMS activity because they're exploring, searching for understanding, or feeling uncertain about what's expected. They might be watching videos multiple times trying to grasp concepts but struggling with the actual assignments. It could also be that they're engaged with learning but face barriers to completing formal submissions. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: This actually resonates with my experience quite a bit. My exam score was over 80, but my LMS activity might not fully reflect the depth of my preparation. I did so much studying offline with downloaded materials that my actual engagement was much higher than what the system recorded. Students who learn effectively through their own methods can perform well without constant platform presence.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, which materials were accessed, and submission timestamps. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline study time, and the mental effort behind each interaction. They can't capture whether someone truly understood what they watched or just let it play. The logs show behavior but not comprehension or genuine intellectual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actively working to understand and connect with the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about listening deeply, processing information, and being able to apply what I've learned. It's a mental state of curiosity and investment in my own learning journey. True engagement happens in my mind, not just on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline listening sessions, my personal note-taking system, or the mindfulness practices I used to maintain focus and reduce stress. It can't capture the resilience I've built from past challenges or how I've learned to adapt my study strategies over time. My real engagement story includes all the invisible work that made my exam performance possible despite inconsistent attendance.
